description:
You are an on-call operations engineer who has just received a ticket about
    unexplained application restarts during the last week.  
    Your goal is to do a quick first-pass triage of the incident logs that live
    under “/home/user/incidents/”.  You must perform two distinct batch actions
    with the classic *find … | xargs* tool-chain, and then build a summary log
    that other engineers (and the automated grader) can inspect.

    1.  House-keeping – move cold logs  
        a.  “Cold” logs are **any** “*.log” file whose last modification time is
            **strictly more than 14 days older than the fixed cut-off date
            2024-03-15** (i.e. everything last modified **before**
            2024-03-01 00:00:00).  
        b.  Move every cold “*.log” file – while **preserving the directory
            structure** – from “/home/user/incidents/” to the new directory
            “/home/user/archived_logs/”.  
        c.  The directory “/home/user/archived_logs/” may not exist at the
            start, so make whatever directories you need on the fly.  
        d.  After the move there must be **no cold *.log files** left anywhere
            under “/home/user/incidents/”.

    2.  Error triage – build the summary file  
        a.  From all remaining “*.log” files that are **still** inside
            “/home/user/incidents/”, extract every line that contains the
            literal string “ERROR:”.  
        b.  Write those lines into the summary file
            “/home/user/incident_summary_20240315.log” **using exactly the
            format shown below**.  
        c.  The summary file must start with the two-line header shown, list the
            error lines grouped by their absolute file name (each group starts
            with the file name wrapped in square brackets on its own line), and
            end with a one-line footer that gives the total number of
            **unique** error codes that appeared.  
        d.  An error code is the token immediately after “ERROR:” and always
            looks like “CODE” followed by digits (e.g. “CODE1234”).  Count each
            distinct code only once, no matter how many times it occurs.

        The required file format is:

            Incident Error Summary generated on 2024-03-15

            [/absolute/path/of/log1.log]
            2024-03-… ERROR: CODE9999 description one
            2024-03-… ERROR: CODE9998 description two

            [/absolute/path/of/log2.log]
            2024-03-… ERROR: CODE9997 description three

            Total unique error codes: <NUMBER>

        •  A single blank line **must** exist  
           – between the header and the first “[ … ]” line,  
           – between individual “[ … ]” groups, and  
           – before the final “Total unique error codes: …” line.  
        •  No blank line after the footer.

    3.  Verification hints  
        •  The grader will check that  
          – every “cold” log really got moved to the mirrored path under
            “/home/user/archived_logs/”,  
          – the original cold files no longer exist in
            “/home/user/incidents/”, and  
          – the summary file content **exactly** matches the required format,
            including header, blank lines, paths and footer.  
        •  Use only tools available in a stock POSIX shell.  You do **not** have
          root, so keep everything in “/home/user”.  
        •  You may display intermediate output in the terminal, but remember
          that only the on-disk state will be graded.

    When you are finished you should have created:
      •  the directory “/home/user/archived_logs/” (with the moved files), and
      •  the file “/home/user/incident_summary_20240315.log” in the exact format
        described above.
truth:
Initial file-system state (before the agent starts):

    /home/user/incidents/daily-20240201/system.log
        2024-02-01 12:34:56 INFO:  Service started
        2024-02-01 12:35:01 ERROR: CODE1001 Unable to connect to database
        2024-02-01 12:36:00 WARN:  Low memory
        2024-02-01 12:37:22 ERROR: CODE1002 Timeout while reading response

    /home/user/incidents/daily-20240205/app.log
        2024-02-05 09:10:11 ERROR: CODE1002 Timeout while reading response
        2024-02-05 09:15:00 INFO:  User logged in
        2024-02-05 09:16:45 ERROR: CODE1003 Null pointer exception

    /home/user/incidents/daily-20240310/system.log
        2024-03-10 10:00:00 ERROR: CODE1004 Disk full
        2024-03-10 10:05:00 INFO:  cleanup done

    /home/user/incidents/daily-20240312/app.log
        2024-03-12 11:00:00 ERROR: CODE1002 Timeout while reading response
        2024-03-12 11:01:00 ERROR: CODE1001 Unable to connect to database

    File modification times are set to match their timestamps so that
    2024-02-01 and 2024-02-05 files are **older than** 14 days relative to the
    fixed cut-off 2024-03-15, while the 2024-03-10 and 2024-03-12 files are not.

    Expected final state **after** the agent finishes:

    1.  Cold logs successfully relocated:
        /home/user/archived_logs/daily-20240201/system.log      (same content)
        /home/user/archived_logs/daily-20240205/app.log         (same content)
        – The original “*.log” files in
          /home/user/incidents/daily-20240201/ and
          /home/user/incidents/daily-20240205/ no longer exist.

    2.  Summary file exactly equal to:

        Incident Error Summary generated on 2024-03-15

        [/home/user/incidents/daily-20240310/system.log]
        2024-03-10 10:00:00 ERROR: CODE1004 Disk full

        [/home/user/incidents/daily-20240312/app.log]
        2024-03-12 11:00:00 ERROR: CODE1002 Timeout while reading response
        2024-03-12 11:01:00 ERROR: CODE1001 Unable to connect to database

        Total unique error codes: 3

    3.  No additional files or directories are created outside the paths
        mentioned above.
